name: Social Proof
summary: Patterns for testimonials, logos, reviews, and trust signals that build credibility and reduce purchase anxiety.

patterns.name: Customer logos
patterns.description: A row of recognizable customer logos that instantly communicates credibility. Most effective for B2B products where brand association signals trust.
do: Show 6-8 logos — enough for credibility, not so many they're overwhelming
Use grayscale/monochrome versions for visual consistency
Include logos the target audience will recognize (industry-relevant)
Place near the hero section — early on the page
Update regularly to show current, relevant customers
dont: Mix colored logos with different styles (visual chaos)
Show logos of companies that aren't actually customers
patterns.evidence: Customer logos increase trust for B2B buyers by 15-25%. Recognition of even one logo significantly increases credibility perception.

patterns.name: Testimonials with attribution
patterns.description: Quotes from real customers with full attribution (name, title, company, photo). The more specific and verifiable, the more trustworthy.
do: Include real name, title, company, and headshot photo
Feature specific outcomes ('Reduced support tickets by 40%')
Rotate testimonials relevant to the page context
Place near decision points (pricing, signup, checkout)
Video testimonials for high-stakes purchases
dont: Write testimonials that sound like marketing copy
patterns.evidence: Testimonials with photos increase trust by 35%. Specific testimonials with metrics are 2x more persuasive than general praise.

patterns.name: Aggregate metrics
patterns.description: Large numbers that communicate scale and momentum: user counts, transactions processed, countries served, uptime percentages.
do: Show 2-3 impressive metrics prominently
Use real, verifiable numbers
Round to readable numbers ('50,000+' not '49,847')
Update regularly to show growth
Choose metrics that resonate with the target audience's concerns
dont: Show vanity metrics that don't indicate trust or value
Use numbers so large they're unrelatable
Show metrics that could be embarrassingly small
Lie or exaggerate — it destroys trust when discovered
patterns.evidence: Aggregate metrics create 'bandwagon effect' — people trust what others trust. '50,000+ teams' is more persuasive than any individual testimonial.

patterns.name: Star ratings and reviews
patterns.description: Numerical ratings and user-generated reviews. Most effective for consumer products and marketplaces where peer experience matters.
do: Show average rating + total number of reviews
Display a distribution chart (5-star breakdown)
Allow filtering by rating
Show recent reviews prominently
Respond to negative reviews publicly (shows accountability)
dont: Show only 5-star reviews (suspicious — 4.2-4.7 is the trust sweet spot)
Show ratings without the review count
Use ratings without a sufficient sample size
patterns.evidence: Products with 4.2-4.5 star ratings convert better than 5.0 (imperfect feels more authentic). Showing the review count increases trust by 20%.

checklist: Is there social proof above the fold or early on the page?
Are customer logos real and permission-granted?
Do testimonials include full attribution (name, title, company, photo)?
Are testimonials specific (metrics, outcomes) not vague?
Are aggregate metrics real and regularly updated?
Is social proof placed near decision points (pricing, signup)?
Are star ratings shown with review counts?
Is there a mix of social proof types (logos + testimonials + metrics)?
Are testimonials relevant to the page context?
Is there social proof that resonates with each target audience segment?
